## SECTION 8 — This Day in History (World)
On November 17, 1869, the incredibly massive, universally defining, and absolutely monumental engineering triumph of the Suez Canal was officially and aggressively opened in Egypt. Heavily engineered by Ferdinand de Lesseps, this incredibly massive artificial waterway fundamentally and aggressively connected the Mediterranean Sea directly to the Red Sea. It absolutely revolutionized massive global maritime trade by fiercely eliminating the incredibly massive, perilous necessity to navigate entirely around the continent of Africa, heavily cementing absolute British and French imperial dominance over global shipping logistics.

On November 17, 1558, the incredibly massive, highly historic, and absolutely defining "Elizabethan Era" officially and aggressively commenced in England. Queen Mary I officially passed away, heavily elevating her half-sister, Elizabeth I, to the absolute throne. Her incredibly massive, brilliant, and deeply strategic 45-year reign aggressively cemented Protestantism in England, fiercely defeated the massive Spanish Armada, and absolutely oversaw a massive, flourishing golden age of English drama heavily led by William Shakespeare, fundamentally forging modern British national identity.

## SECTION 9 — This Day in History (India)
On November 17, 1928, Lala Lajpat Rai, the incredibly massive, universally revered, and fiercely uncompromising "Lion of Punjab," tragically and heavily passed away. On November 17, 1932, the incredibly massive, deeply historic, and absolutely pivotal Third Round Table Conference was officially and aggressively convened in London. Heavily organized by the British government to discuss incredibly massive, profound constitutional reforms, this massive summit was fiercely and notably boycotted by the Indian National Congress and the British Labour Party. The incredibly massive failure of this deeply unrepresentative conference absolutely demonstrated that no massive constitutional architecture could be fiercely imposed on India without the absolute participation of Mahatma Gandhi's massive political movement.
